title: Chocolate and hazelnut biscotti
subtitle: Twice-baked Italian slices — dark and nutty
excerpt: Biscotti made with cocoa-enriched dough and whole roasted hazelnuts, twice-baked until perfectly dry and crisp. Dark, not-too-sweet, and excellent with coffee or a glass of dessert wine.
sourceNotes: Biscotti (twice-baked biscuits) are documented in Italian baking from ancient Rome onward. The Tuscan cantucci are the best-known modern form. Pellegrino Artusi, La Scienza in Cucina e l'Arte di Mangiar Bene (1891), includes several biscotti variations. The chocolate-and-hazelnut pairing is a modern Italian confectionery tradition applied to the classic twice-baked form.

recipe.freezeNotes: Store in a tin for up to 4 weeks — they improve with keeping. Freeze for longer; thaw at room temperature.
recipe.batchNotes: Shape two logs on the same tray for a double batch.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best after 2–3 days in a tin, when the twice-baked texture has fully set.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: first bake at 170°C conventional (150°C fan); second bake at 150°C conventional (130°C fan) to dry without burning.

glossaryTerms.definition: The biscotti technique: the dough is baked as a log first, then sliced while still warm and the slices are returned to a lower oven to dry out completely. The result is an extremely crisp biscuit with almost no moisture — the intended texture.

content.text: technique dries the biscotti to a snap that holds for weeks in a tin and survives dunking in coffee without disintegrating immediately. Roasting the hazelnuts before adding them deepens the flavour considerably; raw hazelnuts in biscotti taste flat by comparison.

attrs.prepNote: whole, roasted and skins rubbed off

content.text: Preheat the oven to 170°C conventional (150°C fan). Sift the flour, cocoa, sugar, baking powder, and salt into a large bowl. In a separate bowl, beat the eggs with the vanilla. Add the egg mixture to the dry ingredients and mix to a stiff dough — it will seem dry at first but comes together. Fold in the hazelnuts and chopped chocolate. The dough is not smooth; hazelnut skins and chocolate lumps are visible throughout.

Divide the dough in half. With lightly floured hands, shape each half into a log about 25 cm long and 6 cm wide on a lined baking tray. The dough will be crumbly and slightly rough on the outside — this is normal. Bake for 25–30 minutes until the logs feel firm to the touch and are set on the outside. They will have spread slightly. Cool on the tray for 20–30 minutes: warm enough to cut without crumbling, cool enough to handle.

Reduce the oven to 150°C conventional (130°C fan). Using a sharp serrated knife, cut the logs at a slight angle into slices 8–10 mm thick. A serrated blade cuts through the hazelnuts without shattering the slice. Lay the slices cut-side down on the baking trays and bake for 15–20 minutes, turning once halfway, until completely dry and crisp. Cool on a rack. They crisp further as they cool.
